The Dimension class — Base class for handling dimensions¶

New in version 0.1.

Module author: Claude Gibert <claude.gibert@synopticview.co.uk>

class slicer.dimensions.Dimension(object)¶

Public methods¶

Dimension(name,officialName,variable,metadata_handler = None):
Parameters:
  • name (string) – name of the dimension. Typically the name of the variable in a netCDF file
  • officialName (string) – name used by the system to identify this variable, for example latitude can be used officially but the name of the variable in a netCDF file could be lat.
  • variable (array) – the array describing the dimension.
  • metadata_handler – a function or callable object which receives the dimension object and a string naming the metadata and returns the value (e.g. slicer.cf10.cf10metadata.CF10Metadata)

Build the Dimension object.

name(newname = None):

return the name of the dimension. If newname is specified, replaces the name with newname and returns it.

officialName(newname = None):

return the official name of the dimension. If newname is specified, replaces the official name with newname and returns it.

metadata(name):

Calls the metadata handler passed at creation time with the parameter name and returns the result.

slice(aSlice):
Parameters:aSlice (slice) – a slice to extract from the dimension.

The slice method returns a slice of the data of the dimension, for example, if the dimension is a latitude, returns the list of the latitudes in that slice. This is used by the slicer.slicer.Slicer to build meta data:

import numpy as np
from semperpy.slicing.dimensions.dimension import Dimension

variable = np.array([12,13,14,15,16,17,18,19,20])
dim = Dimension('dimension','official',variable)

v = dim.slice(slice(1,4,1))
print 'slice:',v
-> slice: [13, 14, 15]
findSlice(values):
Parameters:values (list or dictionary) – values in contents space to find in the dimension

This method returns a list of slice objects corresponding to the requested values:

import numpy as np
from semperpy.slicing.dimensions.dimension import Dimension

variable = np.array([12,13,14,15,16,17,18,19,20])
dim = Dimension('dimension','official',variable)

s = dim.findSlice([12,20])
print s
-> [slice(0, 1, 1), slice(8, 9, 1)]
s = dim.findSlice({13:17})
print s
-> [slice(1, 6, 1)]
__str__():

Implements a neat printing of the bounds of the dimension.

limits():

returns the first and last elements of the variable holding the values of the dimension.

contains(value):
Parameters:value (same type as dimension array elements) – a particular value

returns whether a value is in the dimension or not.

Warning :some implementations of dimensions (e.g. indexed or sorted dimensions) assume that is a value is between its lower and higher bound it is in the dimension although the actual value may not be in there.
find(value):
Parameters:value (same type as dimension array elements) – a particular value

searches the value in the dimension. As opposed to the method contains, this method only returns True is the value is in the dimension. This is a systematic search and it can therefore be costly for large dimensions.

Private methods¶

__call__(values,is_interval):
Parameters:
  • values (list) – values in contents space to find in the dimension
  • is_interval (Boolean) – True is values is an interval (in that case it is a list of 2 elements), False if values contains a list of discrete values

This method must return a list of slice objects corresponding to the requested values.

Note :although findSlice is the method to call, __call__ is the method to redefine if you create a new dimension inheriting from Dimension
hash_slice(aSlice):

Used internally, creates hashing values for slices (which are not hashable) to be able to store them in a dictionary.

extract_slice(aSlice):

Actually extracts a slice and return a list of the value(s). This is called by the method slice but it can be redefined if necessary.
